---
title: "Exercise 1 A: SpatialExperiment"
format: html
editor: source
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this exercise, you will be able to:

-   Understand the structure of a `SpatialExperiment` object.
-   Access and interpret `spatialCoords` and `imgData`.
-   Inspect assay, row, and column metadata.
-   Perform basic subsetting operations on `SpatialExperiment` objects.
-   Visualize Visium HD binned data on the tissue image.

## Libraries

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-1}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| output: false

library(downlit)
library(xml2)
library(GenomeInfoDb)
library(scrapper)
library(SingleR)
library(BayesSpace)
library(xgboost)
library(namer)
library(styler)
```

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-2}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| output: false

library(SpatialExperiment)
library(VisiumIO)
library(ggspavis)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)
library(scuttle)
library(HDF5Array)
```

## Data for the course

We will work with the **Visium HD, sample P2 CRC** dataset from the human colorectal cancer study by Oliveira et al. The public 10x Genomics page also provides a matching Xenium dataset from a serial section of the same sample, which we will use in Exercise 1 B.

The full Visium HD output is very large. For the practical course, we use the 16 um binned output and a precomputed subset generated by `data/prepare_2026_course_data.qmd`.

The subset is chosen for two reasons:

-   it keeps the object small enough for interactive work during the course;
-   it retains a real tissue region that can be compared approximately with the Xenium P2 CRC serial section.

We use an OSTA-style region of interest as the starting point:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-3}
roi_visium <- c(
  xmin = 54000,
  xmax = 58000,
  ymin = 12000,
  ymax = 16000
)
roi_visium
```

The coordinates are in the native Visium HD image coordinate system returned by `spatialCoords()`. This differs from the older P1 exercise, where coordinates were multiplied by `scaleFactors()` to work in low-resolution image coordinates.

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-4}
spe_path <- "data/processed/spe_hd_p2_crc_sub.rds"

if (!file.exists(spe_path)) {
  stop(
    "The prepared Visium HD object was not found. ",
    "Run data/prepare_2026_course_data.qmd first."
  )
}

spe <- readRDS(spe_path)
spe
```

::: callout-important
## Exercise 1

Have a look at the paper and the 10x Genomics website.

-   How are transcript molecules measured in Visium HD?
-   What does the 16 um binned output represent?
-   Why might we use 16 um bins for a course exercise instead of the smallest 2 um bins?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

The measurements of RNA molecules are indirect: targeted probe pairs are hybridized, ligated, and captured for sequencing. The binned Visium HD outputs aggregate molecules over square spatial bins.

The smallest Visium HD binned output is 2 um. The 8 um and 16 um outputs pool neighboring 2 um bins, increasing counts per observation and reducing sparsity at the cost of spatial resolution.

For this course, 16 um bins are a practical compromise. They make the object smaller and faster to plot and process while still preserving tissue-level spatial structure.
:::

## Exploring the object

In this exercise, we will inspect a `SpatialExperiment` object. `SpatialExperiment` extends `SingleCellExperiment`, so familiar accessors such as `colData()`, `rowData()`, `assay()`, and `reducedDims()` still apply.

![Overview of the `SpatialExperiment` class structure](../assets/images/spe.png)

::: callout-important
## Exercise 2

Check the outputs of:

-   `colData()`
-   `rowData()`
-   `assay()`
-   `reducedDims()`

Then check the spatial-specific accessors:

-   `spatialCoords()`
-   `imgData()`
-   `imgRaster()`

Questions:

-   What kind of data is stored in each slot?
-   How many bins and genes are retained in the subsetted `spe` object?
-   Are all retained bins within the tissue area?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

The slot `colData` contains metadata for each spatial bin:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-5}
colData(spe) |> head()
ncol(spe)
```

We can check whether bins are covered by tissue:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-6}
colData(spe) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  group_by(in_tissue) |>
  summarise(number = n())
```

The slot `rowData` contains gene metadata:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-7}
rowData(spe) |> head()
nrow(spe)
```

The assay stores the count matrix:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-8}
assay(spe)
```

The `reducedDims` slot is empty at this stage:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-9}
reducedDims(spe)
```

The `spatialCoords` slot stores image coordinates for each bin:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-10}
spatialCoords(spe) |> head()
```

The `imgData` slot lists the linked tissue image and scale factor:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-11}
imgData(spe)
```

The image can be exported as a raster:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-12}
imgRaster(spe) |> plot()
```
:::

## Visualizing the tissue region

To get a visual overview, we use `ggspavis::plotVisium`. First we plot the tissue image without bins:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-13}
plotVisium(spe, spots = FALSE)
```

Then we overlay the 16 um bins:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-14}
plotVisium(spe, point_shape = 22, point_size = 0.5)
```

::: callout-important
## Exercise 3

Why do we see only a specific region of the slide?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

The full Visium HD output is much larger than needed for this introductory exercise. During data preparation we retained one representative tissue region so that the object remains fast to load, plot, and process. This same region is also used as the anchor for selecting a comparable Xenium region in Exercise 1 B.
:::

It is possible to colour bins according to a gene or a column in `colData`, for example `PIGR`:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-15}
plotVisium(spe,
  annotate = "PIGR",
  assay = "counts",
  zoom = TRUE,
  point_size = 1,
  point_shape = 22
)
```

::: callout-important
## Exercise 4

Check the usage of `plotVisium` with `?plotVisium`. Create a plot that zooms into the selected region and colors the bins according to column `array_row`.

In which slot is `array_row` stored, and what does it represent?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

The column `array_row` is stored in `colData`. It represents the row position of each bin in the Visium HD grid.

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-16}
p <- plotVisium(spe, annotate = "array_row", zoom = TRUE, point_shape = 22)
p
```
:::

## Retain only observations that map to tissue

::: callout-important
## Exercise 5

Subset the `SpatialExperiment` object to retain only bins that are within the tissue area.

How many bins were present in `spe`, and how many are left after selecting `in_tissue` bins only?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-17}
sub <- spe[, colData(spe)$in_tissue]
```

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-18}
dim(spe)
dim(sub)
```

The prepared object is expected to contain only bins from a tissue-covered region. If both dimensions are the same, filtering on `in_tissue` does not remove additional bins.
:::

## Save the object

Save the filtered `SpatialExperiment` object for the next steps:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-19}
dir.create("results/day1", showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
saveHDF5SummarizedExperiment(spe,
  dir = "results/day1", prefix = "01.1_spe", replace = TRUE,
  chunkdim = NULL, level = NULL, as.sparse = NA,
  verbose = NA
)
```

Clear your environment:

```{r day1-1a-SpatialExperiment-20}
#| eval: false
rm(list = ls())
gc()
.rs.restartR()
```

::: callout-important
**Key Takeaways:**

-   `SpatialExperiment` is a convenient container for spot- or bin-level spatial transcriptomics data.
-   Visium HD binned outputs can be represented naturally as a `SpatialExperiment`.
-   Course subsets are chosen to balance biological realism with practical runtime.
:::
